model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Duke enters as the consensus #1 team in the country (KenPom #1, Torvik #1, AdjEM +39.17) with a roughly 20-point efficiency advantage over Ohio State (SRS 19.06, 27th nationally), anchored by the nation's best defense that is perfectly positioned to neutralize a Buckeyes offense that, while solid (ORtg 118.6, 35th nationally), runs into a wall against Duke's elite defensive scheme. The loss of starting point guard Caleb Foster to surgery is a legitimate concern — Duke's ACC Tournament games without him were closer than expected — but the Blue Devils still won the conference tournament and possess the deepest, most talented roster in the country, making their ceiling only marginally lower. Ohio State is genuinely dangerous, entering hot with Big Ten battle-testing and a 21-12 record against the 16th-toughest schedule nationally, and analysts specifically flagged this as a potential upset spot, but the Buckeyes' defense ranked 216th nationally (DRtg 108.2) is a catastrophic liability against Duke's top-4 offense. The efficiency gap, roster quality, and market consensus all point decisively to the Blue Devils advancing despite the Foster-shaped asterisk.

reasoningStep.title: Duke vs. Ohio State — Round of 32 East Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Duke is the clear favorite as the nation's #1 team by every major metric despite the significant Caleb Foster injury. Ohio State is a credible 8-seed with Big Ten battle-testing and a hot finish, but their defense ranked 216th nationally cannot contain Duke's elite offense, and the roughly 20-point efficiency gap is historically insurmountable in single-elimination play.
evidence: Duke: KenPom #1, Torvik #1, AdjEM +39.17, AdjOE 127.9 (4th nationally), AdjDE 88.7 (1st nationally) — the nation's top-ranked team on both ends of the floor.

Ohio State 2025-26: 21-12 record (12-8 Big Ten, 8th place), SRS 19.06 (27th nationally), SOS ranked 16th nationally, ORtg 118.6 (35th), DRtg 108.2 (216th) — strong offense but severely deficient defense.

Caleb Foster (Duke starting PG) is out for the 'foreseeable future' after surgery; Duke won the ACC Tournament without Foster and Ngongba, though games were described as 'a bit too close for comfort.'
USA Today bracket analysts flagged Duke vs. Ohio State as a 'recipe for trouble' — 'The Buckeyes are hot, and they can hang with premier teams' — acknowledging legitimate upset potential.

Ohio State was picked over TCU in Round 1 as a 2.5-point favorite with NET #29, wins over Purdue and Iowa late in the season, and Big Ten battle-testing as key strengths, confirming their quality as an 8-seed.

result.text: NCAA bracket predictions: Final Four, upsets and is it Arizona's year? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers March Madness bracket predictions, from hot takes to upsets to Final Four picks Blake Toppmeyer USA TODAY Madness awaits. Good luck as you try to spot the perfect 12 vs. 5 upset pick, so you can lord your Cinderella -spotting skills over your buddies. If you’ll allow me a word of advice as you fill out your brackets: I like the upset potential of the 11-seeds this year much better than the 12-seeds. While we await the games, here are eight burning thoughts and predictions off this NCAA Tournament bracket . March Madness region breakdown : South | East | Midwest | West Duke is No. 1 team, but Arizona is frontrunner after good region draw The selection committee did Duke dirty. The Blue Devils earned the No. 1 overall seed, but big whoop. Their prize? Getting placed into a region that includes an all-star cast of coaches. Lined up in Duke's way are Dan Hurley, Tom Izzo, Bill Self and Rick Pitino. That’s some kind of reward for the tournament's top seed. Upset specials: Eight March Madness Cinderella teams you'll want to consider in your March Madness bracket Toppmeyer: Six reasons why we love March Madness As if Duke hadn’t absorbed enough bad luck already with the recent injury to point guard Caleb Foster , now it’s got to solve a who’s who list of coaches. The true seeding affirms Duke’s East Region has the best collection of Nos. 1 through 4 seeds. Duke already played and beat Michigan State and Kansas in the regular season. That doesn’t mean it would relish a rematch. Even Duke’s second-round game, potentially against No. 8 Ohio State, brews a recipe for trouble. The Buckeyes are hot, and they can hang with premier teams. They recently beat Purdue, and they gave Michigan a 40-minute fight in the Big Ten tournament. In fact, Duke might prefer to teleport into the West Region, where Arizona is the No. 1 seed. With Duke facing the region of doom, Arizona should be viewed as tournament frontrunners. Arizona already emerged on top of the nation’s best conference. Now, the Wildcats must exorcise some postseason demons. They last reached the Final Four in 2001, with a bundle of flops since then. Better to face old demons, though, than face the teams in Duke’s way of the Final Four. Think you can beat our expert? Join USA TODAY’s Bracket Challenge today! Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! Miami Ohio deserved its NCAA Tournament bid M

Jon Scheyer updates By Shelby Swanson Updated March 15, 2026 8:08 PM Key Takeaways Key Takeaways AI-generated summary reviewed by our newsroom. Read our AI Policy. Scheyer: Foster had right-foot surgery; unlikely to play before Final Four. Ngongba improving with therapy but remains day-by-day, no firm timeline. Injuries could affect NCAA seeding; Boozer had back-to-back 16-point games. Charlotte Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba watched from the bench in matching black hoodies and boots on their right feet as No. 1 seed Duke defeated No. 2 seed Virginia, 74-70, to win the ACC Championship on Saturday night at Charlotte’s Spectrum Center. After the game, Duke coach Jon Scheyer offered an update on both players as the No. 1 Blue Devils turn their attention to the NCAA Tournament. While Foster’s recovery is going to “be a little while still,” Scheyer said he felt “very positive” about Ngongba’s improvement but has no firm timeline on his return. result.text: NCAA bracket predictions: Final Four, upsets and is it Arizona's year? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers March Madness bracket predictions, from hot takes to upsets to Final Four picks Blake Toppmeyer USA TODAY Madness awaits. Good luck as you try to spot the perfect 12 vs. 5 upset pick, so you can lord your Cinderella -spotting skills over your buddies. If you’ll allow me a word of advice as you fill out your brackets: I like the upset potential of the 11-seeds this year much better than the 12-seeds. While we await the games, here are eight burning thoughts and predictions off this NCAA Tournament bracket . March Madness region breakdown : South | East | Midwest | West Duke is No. 1 team, but Arizona is frontrunner after good region draw The selection committee did Duke dirty. The Blue Devils earned the No. 1 overall seed, but big whoop. Their prize? Getting placed into a region that includes an all-star cast of coaches. Lined up in Duke's way are Dan Hurley, Tom Izzo, Bill Self and Rick Pitino. That’s some kind of reward for the tournament's top seed. Upset specials: Eight March Madness Cinderella teams you'll want to consider in your March Madness bracket Toppmeyer: Six reasons why we love March Madness As if Duke hadn’t absorbed enough bad luck already with the recent injury to point guard Caleb Foster , now it’s got to solve a who’s who list of coaches. The true seeding affirms Duke’s East Region has the best collection of Nos. 1 through 4 seeds. Duke already played and beat Michigan State and Kansas in the regular season. That doesn’t mean it would relish a rematch. Even Duke’s second-round game, potentially against No. 8 Ohio State, brews a recipe for trouble. The Buckeyes are hot, and they can hang with premier teams. They recently beat Purdue, and they gave Michigan a 40-minute fight in the Big Ten tournament. In fact, Duke might prefer to teleport into the West Region, where Arizona is the No. 1 seed. With Duke facing the region of doom, Arizona should be viewed as tournament frontrunners. Arizona already emerged on top of the nation’s best conference. Now, the Wildcats must exorcise some postseason demons. They last reached the Final Four in 2001, with a bundle of flops since then. Better to face old demons, though, than face the teams in Duke’s way of the Final Four. Think you can beat our expert? Join USA TODAY’s Bracket Challenge today! Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! Miami Ohio deserved its NCAA Tournament bid M

Scheyer: Foster had right-foot surgery; unlikely to play before Final Four. Ngongba improving with therapy but remains day-by-day, no firm timeline. Injuries could affect NCAA seeding; Boozer had back-to-back 16-point games. Charlotte Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba watched from the bench in matching black hoodies and boots on their right feet as No. 1 seed Duke defeated No. 2 seed Virginia, 74-70, to win the ACC Championship on Saturday night at Charlotte’s Spectrum Center. After the game, Duke coach Jon Scheyer offered an update on both players as the No. 1 Blue Devils turn their attention to the NCAA Tournament.
